items.question: Who uses precision, recall, and F1 in practice?
items.answer: ML engineers, data scientists, product owners, and clinicians evaluating classifiers—anyone who must explain false alarms vs missed detections at a chosen deployment threshold on labeled holdout data.

items.question: What is the F1 score?
items.answer: F1 is the harmonic mean of precision and recall: 2 × (precision × recall) / (precision + recall), computed at one fixed threshold so neither false positives nor false negatives can be ignored when one rate is near zero.

items.question: When should you prioritize recall over precision?
items.answer: When missing a positive case is costlier than a false alarm—screening for serious disease, safety-critical defect detection, or catching fraud rings where false negatives carry legal or health risk.

items.question: Where do TP and FP enter the precision formula?
items.answer: Precision = TP / (TP + FP): the denominator is all examples predicted positive (true positives plus false positives), measuring correctness among positive predictions at the chosen threshold.

items.question: Why use F1 instead of the arithmetic mean of precision and recall?
items.answer: The harmonic mean is pulled down by the smaller rate—if recall is 0.10 and precision is 1.0, arithmetic mean is 0.55 but F1 is 0.18, exposing that the model misses most positives.

items.question: How do you compute precision and recall in scikit-learn?
items.answer: Pass held-out `y_true` and `y_pred` (or thresholded scores) to `precision_score` and `recall_score` with `pos_label` set; use `f1_score` or `classification_report` for the combined metric and per-class breakdown.

items.question: How does precision differ from recall?
items.answer: Precision conditions on predicted positives (TP/(TP+FP))—are our alerts correct? Recall conditions on actual positives (TP/(TP+FN))—did we catch the cases? Lowering the threshold usually raises recall and lowers precision.

items.question: How does macro-F1 differ from micro-F1?
items.answer: Macro-F1 averages per-class F1 with equal weight, surfacing rare-class failure. Micro-F1 pools TP/FP/FN globally then computes F1, matching overall accuracy for single-label multiclass and favoring frequent classes.

items.question: What if you lower the classification threshold from 0.7 to 0.3?
items.answer: More examples are predicted positive, so recall typically rises (fewer FN) while precision typically falls (more FP); F1 may rise or fall depending on where the prior operating point sat on the precision–recall curve.

items.question: What causes precision to collapse while recall stays high?
items.answer: A very low threshold or a model that flags most examples as positive produces many false positives—recall can approach 1 while precision approaches the positive base rate in the dataset.
